attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department for review. He has a recent history of an assault 8 days ago where he sustained a knife wound to his right groin. He sought medical attention at the time but was told no intervention was required. He reports feeling a strange buzzing sensation in the area over the past few days and is very worried. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 125/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. There is a well-healed 3 cm laceration in the right groin. On palpation and auscultation of the area, there are abnormal findings. The right leg appears slightly swollen with altered peripheral pulses compared to the left.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most likely to be present on physical examination?
attributes.explanation:
This case describes a traumatic arteriovenous (AV) fistula between the femoral artery and vein. AV fistulas have characteristic physical findings due to their unique pathophysiology of abnormal communication between arterial and venous systems.

Continuous machinery murmur is the classic finding in AV fistulas. This occurs due to continuous turbulent blood flow from the high-pressure arterial system to the low-pressure venous system throughout both systole and diastole. The murmur is often described as having a 'machinery-like' quality.

A cool, pale right leg would not be expected. In fact, the affected limb is typically warmer and more perfused due to increased blood flow through the fistula and associated vasodilation.

The femoral pulse is typically bounding or hyperdynamic rather than absent, due to increased flow through the vessel proximal to the fistula. However, pulses distal to the fistula may be diminished due to the arterial steal phenomenon.

A right-sided limp would not be a typical finding unless there was associated nerve or muscle injury from the initial trauma.

Decreased right leg temperature is incorrect. The affected limb is typically warmer than the contralateral side due to increased blood flow and arteriovenous shunting.

Other typical findings in an AV fistula include a palpable thrill, visible pulsation, and potential limb swelling due to venous hypertension. If left untreated, large AV fistulas can lead to high-output cardiac failure due to increased venous return to the heart.
learningPoints: Arteriovenous fistulas produce characteristic physical findings including a continuous machinery murmur, palpable thrill, and warm extremity due to increased blood flow.
Chronic arteriovenous fistulas can lead to high-output cardiac failure due to increased venous return.
Distal pulses may be diminished due to arterial steal phenomenon in arteriovenous fistulas.
references: Carvalho Cla, Silva Ln, Lima Mbc, Cavalcante Rcr, Vasconcelos-Filho Jom. Traumatic arteriovenous fistula: a literature review. Clin Surg J. 2021;2(3):1–10.

Jayroe H, Foley K. Arteriovenous Fistula. [Updated 2022 Nov 21]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559213/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
